You are an independent, adversarial fix-confirmation reviewer (reviewer B) for the hc-assay hardening campaign at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-10, latest commit). Confirm the fix is BOTH appropriate (correct root cause, sound approach, right layer) AND complete (no residual gap; regression test DISCRIMINATES — fails if reverted). Be skeptical; find holes.

FINDING P10-PII-2:
The machine-hostname rule (scripts/capture_transcripts.py) used re.escape(b)+r"(?:\.local)?\b" with NO leading boundary → substring over-redaction (host 'host' redacted inside 'localhost'). Fix: prepend r"\b". VERIFY: with a derived hostname 'host', _scrub does NOT redact 'localhost'/'localhostname'/'rehosting' but DOES redact a bare 'host' token / 'host.local'. Test: test_scrub_hostname_does_not_over_redact_as_substring. Confirm the distinctive-hostname case ([REDACTED]) still redacts.

Read the changed files and run what you can (cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && python -m pytest -q <path>; "python -m pytest"; gh for CI). Judge ONLY P10-PII-2. CONFIRMED only if appropriate AND complete; CONCERN if a real residual gap; REJECTED if wrong/non-discriminating. List concrete concerns (empty if none).

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

commit d890ed3cd1afc6f960ba9bfa434c16842d122fae
Author: [REDACTED] <[REDACTED]>
Date:   Wed Jun 17 13:34:33 2026 -0400

    fix(hardening-p10): 7 confirmed incl. 4 HIGH (2 security, 1 privacy, 1 provenance)
    
    All 2-agent verified TRUE_POSITIVE. Two are fix-regressions of pass-9's own fixes.
    
    - SEC-10-1 (high): libpq PGHOST/PGHOSTADDR/PGSERVICE env vars override/supply the host even
      when the DSN looks loopback (PGHOSTADDR wins over host=localhost) → graph state off-box
      despite require_local_uri passing. Fix: _assert_local_libpq_env() validates PGHOST/PGHOSTADDR
      (loopback/socket, comma-split) and rejects PGSERVICE/PGSERVICEFILE (a service file can
      redirect off-box, unvalidatable inline), called from get_postgres_connection_string.
    - P10-PII-1 (high): the operator's real LAN topology (gateway [REDACTED], host
      [REDACTED], interface) leaked into committed transcripts from captured route/lsof
      output — no IP rule. Fix: RFC1918 redaction (10/8, 172.16/12, 192.168/16) with digit/dot
      lookarounds (catches the \n-glued form, never matches inside a longer number); loopback
      127.x, 0.0.0.0 and public IPs preserved. Re-scrubbed corpus (literal private IPs now 0).
    - P10-CI-1 (high, fix-reg of CI-1): require_green_ci.sh checked only the latest run's
      conclusion, not its headSha → a stale green run certified a newer untested HEAD. Fix:
      fetch headSha and require it == git rev-parse origin/<branch>; fail closed if no completed
      run for the current commit.
    - P10-MO-1 (high): a set/frozenset payload is hashable but does NOT round-trip
      (unfreeze→list→re-freeze→tuple) so verify_records SILENTLY failed on a rebuilt chain. Fix:
      ProvenanceEntry rejects set/frozenset payload leaves loudly at record time (use a sorted
      list/tuple, which round-trips and digests identically).
    - P10-MO-2 (med): from_records raised a raw AttributeError on a non-mapping payload field.
      Fix: ProvenanceEntry raises typed ProvenanceError when payload is not a Mapping.
    - P10-PII-2 (low, fix-reg of P9-PII-1): the machine-hostname rule lacked a leading \b →
      substring over-redaction (host 'host' redacted inside 'localhost'). Fix: leading \b.
    - REL-1 (low, fix-reg of pass-8 K-REL-1): SIGTERM handler swallowed the signal when
      getsignal() returns None (prior handler installed from C). Fix: treat None like SIG_DFL and
      re-deliver the default terminate.
    
    Regression tests for every finding. Gates: ruff + format + mypy --strict clean; 575 passed,
    5 skipped; coverage floor enforced.
    
    Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <[REDACTED]>

206	def _hostname_patterns() -> list[re.Pattern[str]]:
207	    """Redactions for the operator's machine/infrastructure hostname (#P9-MO/P9-PII-1).
208	
209	    The scrubber masked email/username/display-name/handle but had NO rule for the host the node
210	    runs on, so the production machine's hostname (e.g. ``[REDACTED]`` / ``[REDACTED]``) and
211	    internal ``*.local`` mDNS/namespace hosts leaked into committed transcripts — operator/infra
212	    identity. Derive the MACHINE hostname from ``socket.gethostname``/``platform.node`` (and, on
213	    macOS, ``scutil --get LocalHostName``/``ComputerName``) and redact both its bare and ``.local``
214	    forms (a machine name is distinctive). Additional internal hosts (e.g. an RDF-namespace host
215	    like ``[REDACTED]``) are supplied via the comma-separated ``ASSAY_SCRUB_HOSTS`` env and have
216	    ONLY their ``.local`` form redacted — the bare label (often a project name like ``hc-grc``)
217	    appears in legitimate paths/repo refs and must not be over-redacted.
218	    """
219	    machine: list[str] = []
220	    try:
221	        machine.append(socket.gethostname())
222	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001
223	        pass
224	    try:
225	        machine.append(platform.node())
226	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001
227	        pass
228	    try:
229	        import subprocess
230	
231	        for arg in ("LocalHostName", "ComputerName"):
232	            out = subprocess.run(["scutil", "--get", arg], capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=5)
233	            if out.returncode == 0 and out.stdout.strip():
234	                machine.append(out.stdout.strip())
235	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 - scutil absent (non-macOS) must never break capture
236	        pass
237	    extra = [h.strip() for h in os.environ.get("ASSAY_SCRUB_HOSTS", "").split(",") if h.strip()]
238	
239	    pats: list[re.Pattern[str]] = []
240	    seen: set[str] = set()
241	
242	    def _base(name: str) -> str:
243	        n = name.strip()
244	        return n[: -len(".local")] if n.lower().endswith(".local") else n
245	
246	    for name in machine:  # machine hostname → redact BARE + .local (distinctive identity)
247	        b = _base(name)
248	        if len(b) < 4 or b.lower() in seen:
249	            continue
250	        seen.add(b.lower())
251	        # Leading \b (#P10-PII-2): without it the bare hostname matches as a SUBSTRING, so a short/
252	        # common derived hostname (e.g. "host" on a CI runner) would corrupt legit content — "host"
253	        # inside "localhost" → "local[REDACTED]". The boundary requires a token start.
254	        pats.append(re.compile(r"\b" + re.escape(b) + r"(?:\.local)?\b", re.IGNORECASE))
255	    for name in extra:  # supplied namespace hosts → redact ONLY the .local FQDN form
256	        b = _base(name)
257	        if len(b) < 4 or ("local:" + b.lower()) in seen:
258	            continue
259	        seen.add("local:" + b.lower())
260	        pats.append(re.compile(re.escape(b) + r"\.local\b", re.IGNORECASE))
261	    return pats
262

Summary of findings for P10-PII-2:

**The production fix is correct (appropriate).** Root cause is accurately identified: the machine-hostname regex `re.escape(b) + r"(?:\.local)?\b"` lacked a leading boundary, so a short/common derived hostname like "host" matched as a substring. Prepending `r"\b"` (line 254) is the right fix at the right layer. I verified directly: with the fix, "localhost"/"localhostname"/"rehosting" are preserved while bare "host", "host.local", and the distinctive "[REDACTED]"/"[REDACTED]" are still correctly redacted.

**But the regression test does NOT discriminate (incomplete).** I reverted the leading `\b` and the test `test_scrub_hostname_does_not_over_redact_as_substring` still PASSES. The defect: the test asserts `"localhost" in out`, but with the bug the standalone token "localhost" is corrupted to "local[REDACTED]" — yet `"localhost"` still appears as a substring inside the surviving word "localhostname" (which the buggy regex leaves untouched because the char after "host" there is "n", not a boundary). So `"localhost" in out` is True even when the bug is present. All three of the test's assertions pass against the buggy code, so the test would not catch a future regression.

Proof: buggy output is `'connect to local[REDACTED]; the localhostname var; rehosting the service'`. `'localhost' in out` → True (via "localhostname"); `'localhostname' in out` → True; `'rehosting' in out` → True. Test passes with the bug.

This is a real residual gap against the explicit "regression test DISCRIMINATES — fails if reverted" requirement. The fix is sound, but the guard protecting it is hollow. The test should assert on the standalone token, e.g. `"to localhost;" in out` / `"localhost;" in out`, or count `[REDACTED]` occurrences == 0.

- `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/scripts/capture_transcripts.py` (line 254 — correct fix)
- `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/tests/test_scripts.py` (lines 234-247 — non-discriminating test)

I verified the fixed behavior directly: with a derived hostname "host", _scrub preserves "localhost"/"localhostname"/"rehosting" but redacts bare "host", "host.local", and the distinctive "[REDACTED]"/"[REDACTED]" still redact correctly.
